subject: ap-government
title: Quantitative Analysis — Composition of Federal Spending, Fiscal Years 1962–2019
stimulus: Source: Tax Policy Center, 2020. Line graph showing Composition of Federal Spending for Fiscal Years 1962–2019, displaying trends in mandatory spending, discretionary spending, and net interest as percentages of the total federal budget.

parts.prompt: Identify the type of spending that comprised the greatest percentage of the federal budget in 2016.
parts.scoring_notes: Only mandatory spending earns the point.

scoring_points.description: Identify the type of spending that comprised the greatest percentage of the federal budget in 2016.

parts.prompt: Describe the difference in the trends in mandatory versus discretionary spending as illustrated in the graph.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must describe both trends (mandatory increasing, discretionary decreasing) as a contrasting pattern. Describing only one trend does not earn the point.

scoring_points.description: Describe the difference in the trends in mandatory versus discretionary spending as illustrated in the graph.

required_elements: As a percentage of total federal spending, mandatory spending increased whereas during that same time frame discretionary spending decreased.
alternatives.correct_example: As a percentage of total federal spending, mandatory spending increased whereas during that same time frame discretionary spending decreased.

parts.prompt: Draw a conclusion about Congress's budgetary choices based on trends in the data.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must draw a conclusion (an inference about what Congress is choosing to do), not just describe the data. Accept any plausible conclusion about Congressional budgetary behavior supported by the trends.

scoring_points.description: Draw a conclusion about Congress's budgetary choices based on trends in the data.

required_elements: Mandatory spending increases over time, so Congress decreases discretionary spending.
alternatives.correct_example: Mandatory spending increases over time, so Congress decreases discretionary spending.

required_elements: Mandatory spending increases over time, so Congress raises taxes.
alternatives.correct_example: Mandatory spending increases over time, so Congress raises taxes.

required_elements: Mandatory spending increases over time, so Congress continues similar funding levels and increases the budget deficit.
alternatives.correct_example: Mandatory spending increases over time, so Congress continues similar funding levels and increases the budget deficit.

parts.prompt: Explain how an interest group might respond to trends shown in the graph.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must explain a specific response connected to the spending trends — not just state that interest groups respond to spending. Accept any plausible and specific interest group response.

scoring_points.description: Explain how an interest group might respond to trends shown in the graph.

required_elements: As discretionary spending decreases, interest groups might be more motivated to fight for the survival of policies they support.
alternatives.correct_example: As discretionary spending decreases, interest groups might be more motivated to fight for the survival of policies they support.

required_elements: Interest groups might lobby for changes to programs funded by mandatory spending that would benefit their members.
alternatives.correct_example: Interest groups might lobby for changes to programs funded by mandatory spending that would benefit their members.

required_elements: Interest groups may lobby more aggressively for programs funded by discretionary spending since discretionary spending is decreasing as a percentage of the total budget.
alternatives.correct_example: Interest groups may lobby more aggressively for programs funded by discretionary spending since discretionary spending is decreasing as a percentage of the total budget.
